title: Grafton (town), Wisconsin

infobox.value: Supervisors   * Tom Grabow * Paul Melotik * Tom Sykora * Karron Stockwell

is a [town](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_Wisconsin#town "Administrative divisions of Wisconsin") located in [Ozaukee County, Wisconsin](/wiki/Ozaukee_County,_Wisconsin "Ozaukee County, Wisconsin"), United States, and is in the [Milwaukee metropolitan area](/wiki/Milwaukee_metropolitan_area "Milwaukee metropolitan area"). The town was created in 1846 and at the time of the [2020 Census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census") had a population of 4,157.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Town in Ozaukee County, Wisconsin

This article is about the Town of Grafton. For the adjacent village, see [Grafton, Wisconsin](/wiki/Grafton,_Wisconsin "Grafton, Wisconsin").

Town in Wisconsin, United States

The **Town of Grafton** is a [town](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_Wisconsin#town "Administrative divisions of Wisconsin") located in [Ozaukee County, Wisconsin](/wiki/Ozaukee_County,_Wisconsin "Ozaukee County, Wisconsin"), United States, and is in the [Milwaukee metropolitan area](/wiki/Milwaukee_metropolitan_area "Milwaukee metropolitan area"). The town was created in 1846 and at the time of the [2020 Census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census") had a population of 4,157.

Grafton is located on the western shore of [Lake Michigan](/wiki/Lake_Michigan "Lake Michigan"). German and Irish immigrants first settled in Grafton in the 1840s. Their centers of settlement became the unincorporated communities of [Lakefield](/wiki/Lakefield,_Wisconsin "Lakefield, Wisconsin") and [Ulao](/wiki/Ulao,_Wisconsin "Ulao, Wisconsin"), as well as the [Village](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_Wisconsin#village "Administrative divisions of Wisconsin") of [Grafton](/wiki/Grafton,_Wisconsin "Grafton, Wisconsin"), which is partially located in the town.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grafton_(town),_Wisconsin&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The first Europeans to visit the area were the Jesuit missionaries [Claude-Jean Allouez](/wiki/Claude-Jean_Allouez "Claude-Jean Allouez") and Claude Bablon, who visited a Native American village on the [Milwaukee River](/wiki/Milwaukee_River "Milwaukee River") near the future site of Grafton around the year 1670.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Timothy Wooden, who arrived in 1839 from the eastern United States, is considered Grafton's first permanent resident. The majority of the early residents were immigrants from Germany and Ireland. The [Wisconsin territorial legislature](/wiki/Wisconsin_Territory "Wisconsin Territory") officially created the Town of Grafton in 1846.

James T. Gifford, an investor who founded [Elgin, Illinois](/wiki/Elgin,_Illinois "Elgin, Illinois"), developed the community of [Ulao](/wiki/Ulao,_Wisconsin "Ulao, Wisconsin") in 1847 as a port on [Lake Michigan](/wiki/Lake_Michigan "Lake Michigan"). At the time, [steamships](/wiki/Steamship "Steamship") were common on the Great Lakes and burned massive amounts of wood for fuel. A steamship could consume wood equivalent to several acres worth of forest on single journey. Much of Grafton was primeval beech-maple forest, which settlers were clearing for agriculture, and Gifford saw an opportunity for Ulao to prosper as a steamship refueling station. He built a sawmill, a warehouse, and a one-thousand-foot-long pier on the lake where ships docked to buy wood. The community prospered in the 1850s and 1860s, but by the end of the American Civil War, Ozaukee County's forests had been largely depleted, and Ulao declined, with most of the land being converted to agriculture.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The [Village](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_Wisconsin#Village "Administrative divisions of Wisconsin") of Grafton was incorporated from some of the town's land in 1896.

In the late 1930s, a group of pro-[Nazi](/wiki/Nazi_Party "Nazi Party") German-Americans affiliated with the [German American Bund](/wiki/German_American_Bund "German American Bund") purchased land on the Milwaukee River in the Town of Grafton. They ran a private camp called Camp Hindenburg, and hosted a speech by Nazi-supporter [Fritz Julius Kuhn](/wiki/Fritz_Julius_Kuhn "Fritz Julius Kuhn") in 1939.[[5]](#cite_note-5) The camp closed with the outbreak of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") in 1941. George Froboese, a prominent member of the camp, committed suicide in 1942 while being escorted to New York to answer a Federal subpoena.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Paul Knauer, another member of the camp, was stripped of his U.S. citizenship for having falsely taken the oath of allegiance and was deported back to Germany after the war.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

In 1940, the pro-American, anti-Bund Wisconsin Federation of German-American Societies opened Camp Carl Schurz in the Town of Grafton to compete with the Nazi-sympathizers.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Grafton experienced significant population growth during the [suburbanization](/wiki/Suburbanization "Suburbanization") that followed World War II, and the village annexed more farm land from the town of Grafton for residential subdivisions and commercial developments. The construction of [Interstate 43](/wiki/Interstate_43 "Interstate 43") in the mid-1960s connected Grafton to other communities, such as [Milwaukee](/wiki/Milwaukee "Milwaukee") and [Sheboygan](/wiki/Sheboygan_County,_Wisconsin "Sheboygan County, Wisconsin").[[11]](#cite_note-11)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grafton_(town),_Wisconsin&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/LionsDen_Oz-Co-WI.png/250px-LionsDen_Oz-Co-WI.png)](/wiki/File:LionsDen_Oz-Co-WI.png)

The clay bluffs on [Lake Michigan](/wiki/Lake_Michigan "Lake Michigan") and the [Port Washington Breakwater Light](/wiki/Port_Washington_Breakwater_Light "Port Washington Breakwater Light") seen from Lion's Den Gorge park in the Town of Grafton.

According to the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), the town has a total area of 21.4 square miles (55 km2), of which, 19.8 square miles (51 km2) of it is land and 1.6 square miles (4.1 km2) of it (7.47%) is water.

The [Village of Grafton](/wiki/Grafton,_Wisconsin "Grafton, Wisconsin") is mostly located in what was formerly the town's western half. The town is also bordered by the [City of Port Washington](/wiki/Port_Washington,_Wisconsin "Port Washington, Wisconsin"), [Town of Port Washington](/wiki/Port_Washington_(town),_Wisconsin "Port Washington (town), Wisconsin"), and [Town of Saukville](/wiki/Saukville_(town),_Wisconsin "Saukville (town), Wisconsin") to the north; the [City of Mequon](/wiki/Mequon,_Wisconsin "Mequon, Wisconsin") to the south; the [City of Cedarburg](/wiki/Cedarburg,_Wisconsin "Cedarburg, Wisconsin") and the [City of Cedarburg](/wiki/Cedarburg_(town),_Wisconsin "Cedarburg (town), Wisconsin") to the west; and [Lake Michigan](/wiki/Lake_Michigan "Lake Michigan") to the east. The unincorporated communities of [Lakefield](/wiki/Lakefield,_Wisconsin "Lakefield, Wisconsin") and [Ulao](/wiki/Ulao,_Wisconsin "Ulao, Wisconsin") are also located in the town.

The town is located on the western shore of [Lake Michigan](/wiki/Lake_Michigan "Lake Michigan"), and the coastline is characterized by clay [bluffs](/wiki/Cliff "Cliff") ranging from 80 to 140 feet (24 to 43 meters) in height with deep [ravines](/wiki/Ravine "Ravine") where streams flow into the lake. Clay bluffs are a geological formation are characteristic of the Lake Michigan shoreline, and are found in few other areas of the world. Much of the coast has mixed gravel and sand beaches.[[12]](#cite_note-Resources-12)[[13]](#cite_note-Coastal-13) The [Kevich Light](/wiki/Kevich_Light "Kevich Light"), a privately owned lighthouse on the lake, is located in the town.

The town is located in the [Southeastern Wisconsin glacial till plains](/wiki/Southeastern_Wisconsin_Till_Plains_(ecoregion) "Southeastern Wisconsin Till Plains (ecoregion)") that were created by the [Wisconsin glaciation](/wiki/Wisconsin_glaciation "Wisconsin glaciation") during the most recent ice age. The soil is clayey glacial [till](/wiki/Till "Till") with a thin layer of [loess](/wiki/Loess "Loess") on the surface. The [Wisconsin Department of Natural Resources](/wiki/Wisconsin_Department_of_Natural_Resources "Wisconsin Department of Natural Resources") considers the eastern part of the town along the lake to be in the Central Lake Michigan Coastal ecological landscape, while the western part of the town is in the Southeast Glacial Plains ecological landscape.[[12]](#cite_note-Resources-12)

Before white settlers arrived in the area, the Grafton area was an upland forest dominated by [American beech](/wiki/Fagus_grandifolia "Fagus grandifolia") and [sugar maple](/wiki/Acer_saccharum "Acer saccharum") trees. There were also [white cedars](/wiki/Thuja_occidentalis "Thuja occidentalis") growing along the [Milwaukee River](/wiki/Milwaukee_River "Milwaukee River"), which flows through Grafton. Much of the original forest was cleared to prepare the land for agriculture.[[12]](#cite_note-Resources-12) The Bratt Woods nature preserve and the Kurtz Woods State Natural Area, which are both maintained by the Ozaukee Washington Land Trust, have old growth endemic trees and retain the character of the pre-settlement beech-maple forests.[[14]](#cite_note-Bratt-14)[[15]](#cite_note-Kurtz-15)

As land development continues to reduce wild areas, wildlife is forced into closer proximity with human communities like Grafton. Large mammals, including [white-tailed deer](/wiki/White-tailed_deer "White-tailed deer"), [coyotes](/wiki/Coyotes "Coyotes"), and [red foxes](/wiki/Red_fox "Red fox") can be seen in the town. Many birds, including [great blue herons](/wiki/Great_blue_heron "Great blue heron") and [wild turkeys](/wiki/Wild_turkey "Wild turkey") are found in the town, and waterfowl including [green herons](/wiki/Green_heron "Green heron") and [American bitterns](/wiki/American_bittern "American bittern") breed in the town's wetlands.[[16]](#cite_note-16) The Bratt Woods nature preserve is a habitat for the [American gromwell](/wiki/Lithospermum_latifolium "Lithospermum latifolium"), a State-designated special concern plant species.[[12]](#cite_note-Resources-12)

The region struggles with many invasive species, including the [emerald ash borer](/wiki/Emerald_ash_borer "Emerald ash borer"), [common carp](/wiki/Common_carp "Common carp"), [reed canary grass](/wiki/Phalaris_arundinacea "Phalaris arundinacea"), the [common reed](/wiki/Phragmites "Phragmites"), [purple loosestrife](/wiki/Lythrum_salicaria "Lythrum salicaria"), [garlic mustard](/wiki/Alliaria_petiolata "Alliaria petiolata"), [Eurasian buckthorns](/wiki/Rhamnus_(plant) "Rhamnus (plant)"), and [honeysuckles](/wiki/Honeysuckle "Honeysuckle").[[13]](#cite_note-Coastal-13)

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grafton_(town),_Wisconsin&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Demographics")]

As of the [census](/wiki/Census "Census")[[17]](#cite_note-GR2-17) of 2000, there were 4,132 people, 1,569 households, and 1,241 families residing in the town. The [population density](/wiki/Population_density "Population density") was 208.5 people per square mile (80.5/km2). There were 1,608 housing units at an average density of 81.1 per square mile (31.3/km2). The racial makeup of the town was 98.02% [White](/wiki/White_(U.S._Census) "White (U.S. Census)"), 0.34% [Black](/wiki/African_American_(U.S._Census) "African American (U.S. Census)") or [African American](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)"), 0.22% [Native American](/wiki/Native_American_(U.S._Census) "Native American (U.S. Census)"), 0.48% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_(U.S._Census) "Asian (U.S. Census)"), 0.10% [Pacific Islander](/wiki/Pacific_Islander_(U.S._Census) "Pacific Islander (U.S. Census)"), 0.34% from [other races](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)"), and 0.51% from two or more races. 1.11% of the population were [Hispanic](/wiki/Hispanic "Hispanic") or [Latino](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans "Hispanic and Latino Americans") of any race.

There were 1,569 households, out of which 33.5% had children under the age of 18 living with them, 71.5% were [married couples](/wiki/Marriage "Marriage") living together, 5.0% had a female householder with no husband present, and 20.9% were non-families. 16.1% of all households were made up of individuals, and 4.8% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older. The average household size was 2.63 and the average family size was 2.98.

In the town, the population was spread out, with 24.7% under the age of 18, 5.9% from 18 to 24, 28.0% from 25 to 44, 31.2% from 45 to 64, and 10.2% who were 65 years of age or older. The median age was 41 years. For every 100 females, there were 104.6 males. For every 100 females age 18 and over, there were 101.2 males.

The median income for a household in the town was $64,707, and the median income for a family was $73,125. Males had a median income of $42,320 versus $35,446 for females. The [per capita income](/wiki/Per_capita_income "Per capita income") for the town was $30,582. About 4.0% of families and 5.1% of the population were below the [poverty line](/wiki/Poverty_line "Poverty line"), including 6.4% of those under age 18 and 8.0% of those age 65 or over.

## Law and government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grafton_(town),_Wisconsin&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Law and government")]

Grafton is organized as a [town](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_Wisconsin#Town "Administrative divisions of Wisconsin") governed by an elected board, comprising a chairman and four supervisors. The current chairman is Lester A. Bartel Jr. The board meets on the second Wednesday of each month at 7 p.m. in the town hall which is located in the Village of Grafton.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

As part of [Wisconsin's 6th congressional district](/wiki/Wisconsin%27s_6th_congressional_district "Wisconsin's 6th congressional district"), Grafton is represented by [Glenn Grothman](/wiki/Glenn_Grothman "Glenn Grothman") (R) in the [United States House of Representatives](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives"), and by [Ron Johnson](/wiki/Ron_Johnson "Ron Johnson") (R) and [Tammy Baldwin](/wiki/Tammy_Baldwin "Tammy Baldwin") (D) in the [United States Senate](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate"). [Alberta Darling](/wiki/Alberta_Darling "Alberta Darling") (R) represents Grafton in the [Wisconsin State Senate](/wiki/Wisconsin_State_Senate "Wisconsin State Senate"), and [Deb Andraca](/wiki/Deb_Andraca "Deb Andraca") (D) represents Grafton in the [Wisconsin State Assembly](/wiki/Wisconsin_State_Assembly "Wisconsin State Assembly").[[19]](#cite_note-19)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grafton_(town),_Wisconsin&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Education")]

Much of the town of Grafton is served by the [Grafton School District](/wiki/Grafton_School_District "Grafton School District"), which has two [elementary schools](/wiki/Elementary_school_(United_States) "Elementary school (United States)"), serving kindergarten through fifth grade: John F. Kennedy Elementary School and Woodview Elementary School. John Long Middle School serves the entire district for grades six through eight, and [Grafton High School](/wiki/Grafton_High_School_(Wisconsin) "Grafton High School (Wisconsin)") serves grades nine through twelve.

The district is governed by a seven-member elected [school board](/wiki/Board_of_education "Board of education"), which meets on the fourth Monday of the month at 6 p.m. in the Grafton High School Library. The current district superintendent, Jeff Nelson, has held the position since 2016.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

Some residents in the southwestern part of the town attend the [Cedarburg School District](/wiki/Cedarburg_School_District "Cedarburg School District"), and residents in the northeastern part of the town attend the [Port Washington-Saukville School District](/wiki/Port_Washington-Saukville_School_District "Port Washington-Saukville School District").[[21]](#cite_note-21)

Grafton also has three [parochial schools](/wiki/Parochial_school "Parochial school") that serve students from kindergarten through eight grade: Our Savior Lutheran School,[[22]](#cite_note-Savior-22) St. Joseph Catholic Parish School,[[23]](#cite_note-Joe-23) and St. Paul Lutheran School.[[24]](#cite_note-Paul-24)

## Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grafton_(town),_Wisconsin&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Transportation")]

[Interstate 43](/wiki/Interstate_43 "Interstate 43") passes through the town with access via Exit 92 and Exit 93, and [Wisconsin Highway 60](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_60 "Wisconsin Highway 60") also passes through [Ulao](/wiki/Ulao,_Wisconsin "Ulao, Wisconsin") and the downtown Village of Grafton.

Grafton has limited public transit compared with larger cities. Ozaukee County and the [Milwaukee County Transit System](/wiki/Milwaukee_County_Transit_System "Milwaukee County Transit System") run the Route 143 commuter bus, also known as the "Ozaukee County Express," to Milwaukee via Interstate 43. The bus makes two stops at park-and-ride lots in the village off of Exit 92: one at the Grafton Commons shopping center and the other in the [Target](/wiki/Target_Corporation "Target Corporation") parking lot. The bus operates Monday through Friday with limited hours corresponding to peak commute times.[[25]](#cite_note-Wisconsin_DOT-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26) Ozaukee County Transit Services' Shared Ride Taxi is the public transit option for traveling to sites not directly accessible from the interstate. The taxis operate seven days a week and make connections to Washington County Transit and Milwaukee County Routes 12, 49 and 42u.[[25]](#cite_note-Wisconsin_DOT-25)[[27]](#cite_note-27)

The [Ozaukee Interurban Trail](/wiki/Ozaukee_Interurban_Trail "Ozaukee Interurban Trail"), which is for pedestrian and bicycle use, goes through both the town and the Village of Grafton, connecting residents to the neighboring communities of [Cedarburg](/wiki/Cedarburg,_Wisconsin "Cedarburg, Wisconsin") and [Port Washington](/wiki/Port_Washington,_Wisconsin "Port Washington, Wisconsin"). The trail continues north to [Sheboygan County](/wiki/Sheboygan_County "Sheboygan County") and south to [Milwaukee County](/wiki/Milwaukee_County "Milwaukee County").

The [Wisconsin Central Ltd.](/wiki/Wisconsin_Central_Ltd. "Wisconsin Central Ltd.") railroad operates a freight rail line which passes through the town and goes north to [Saukville](/wiki/Saukville,_Wisconsin "Saukville, Wisconsin") and south to Cedarburg. The [Union Pacific Railroad](/wiki/Union_Pacific_Railroad "Union Pacific Railroad") runs parallel to Interstate 43.[[28]](#cite_note-28) The town currently does not have a train station.

## Parks and recreation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grafton_(town),_Wisconsin&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Parks and recreation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Bluff_over_Lake_Michigan_-_Lion%27s_Den_Gorge_Nature_Preserve_in_Grafton_Wisconsin.jpg/250px-Bluff_over_Lake_Michigan_-_Lion%27s_Den_Gorge_Nature_Preserve_in_Grafton_Wisconsin.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bluff_over_Lake_Michigan_-_Lion%27s_Den_Gorge_Nature_Preserve_in_Grafton_Wisconsin.jpg)

The clay bluffs on [Lake Michigan](/wiki/Lake_Michigan "Lake Michigan") at Lions Den Gorge Nature Preserve.

* **Blue Wing Waterfowl Production Area**: The [United States Fish and Wildlife Service](/wiki/United_States_Fish_and_Wildlife_Service "United States Fish and Wildlife Service") maintains a 54-acre wetland and prairie preserve which is home to species including green herons and American bitterns.[[29]](#cite_note-29)
* **Bratt Woods**: The Ozaukee Washington Land Trust maintains this 17-acre park on the eastern bank of the [Milwaukee River](/wiki/Milwaukee_River "Milwaukee River"). The forest features endemic plants and retains the character of the beech-maple forests that sprawled across Ozaukee County in the early 19th century.[[14]](#cite_note-Bratt-14) The land is also a habitat for the [American gromwell](/wiki/Lithospermum_latifolium "Lithospermum latifolium"), a state-designated special concern plant species.[[12]](#cite_note-Resources-12)
* **Kurtz Woods Natural Area**: Designated as a state natural area, the Ozaukee Washington Land Trust maintains this 45-acre park, which is split between the Town of Grafton and the Village of Saukville. The preserve boasts 82 documented plant species in a primeval beech-maple forest, and landforms in the park include glacially formed [kettles](/wiki/Kettle_(landform) "Kettle (landform)").[[15]](#cite_note-Kurtz-15)
* **Lion's Den Gorge Nature Preserve**: Ozaukee County maintains this 73-acre park on the shores of [Lake Michigan](/wiki/Lake_Michigan "Lake Michigan") with picnic facilities, walking trails, a beach, and views of the lake from 100-foot-high bluffs.[[30]](#cite_note-30)
* **Ulao Waterfowl Production Area**: The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service maintains a 44-acre waterfowl sanctuary adjacent to Lion's Den Gorge Nature Preserve trails for hiking and opportunities to observe dozens of bird species, including loons, scoters, and ducks.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

The [Ozaukee Interurban Trail](/wiki/Ozaukee_Interurban_Trail "Ozaukee Interurban Trail") runs through the town, following the former route of the [Milwaukee Interurban Rail Line](/wiki/The_Milwaukee_Electric_Railway_and_Light_Company "The Milwaukee Electric Railway and Light Company"). The southern end of the trail is at Bradley Road in [Brown Deer](/wiki/Brown_Deer,_Wisconsin "Brown Deer, Wisconsin") which connects to the [Oak Leaf Trail](/wiki/Oak_Leaf_Trail "Oak Leaf Trail") ([43°09′48″N 87°57′39″W﻿ / ﻿43.16333°N 87.96083°W﻿ / 43.16333; -87.96083](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Grafton_(town),_Wisconsin&params=43_09_48_N_87_57_39_W_region:US-WI)), and its northern end is at DeMaster Road in the Village of [Oostburg](/wiki/Oostburg,_Wisconsin "Oostburg, Wisconsin") [Sheboygan County](/wiki/Sheboygan_County "Sheboygan County") ([43°36′57″N 87°48′08″W﻿ / ﻿43.61583°N 87.80222°W﻿ / 43.61583; -87.80222](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Grafton_(town),_Wisconsin&params=43_36_57_N_87_48_08_W_region:US-WI)). The trail connects the community to neighboring [Cedarburg](/wiki/Cedarburg,_Wisconsin "Cedarburg, Wisconsin") and [Port Washington](/wiki/Port_Washington,_Wisconsin "Port Washington, Wisconsin").

There are also two private, commercial golf courses in the town and the privately owned 20-acre Grafton Dells River Park.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

## Notable people

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grafton_(town),_Wisconsin&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Notable people")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Charles_J_Guiteau.jpg/250px-Charles_J_Guiteau.jpg)](/wiki/File:Charles_J_Guiteau.jpg)

[Charles J. Guiteau](/wiki/Charles_J._Guiteau "Charles J. Guiteau") (1841–1882) assassinated U.S. President [James A. Garfield](/wiki/James_A._Garfield "James A. Garfield") in 1881. Guiteau lived in [Ulao](/wiki/Ulao,_Wisconsin "Ulao, Wisconsin") during his adolescence.[[33]](#cite_note-Hewitt-33)

* [Charles Guiteau](/wiki/Charles_Guiteau "Charles Guiteau"), assassin of President [James Garfield](/wiki/James_Garfield "James Garfield") lived in Ulao from 1850 to 1855[[33]](#cite_note-Hewitt-33)
